message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated using a hierarchical scheme that separates on-task intents (task-specific key actions defined per task) and off-task intents (universal common dialog acts, split into general and social intents).
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

Automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP) and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP). Human evaluation metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length (Length) and Task Success Score (TaskSuc).
Unanswerable
They compare against Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012).
They report accuracy and use the TDT procedure with Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves (miss and false alarm probability), and measure efficiency by throughput per second.

Yes — they frame rumour detection as a classification task and learn feature weights and the detection threshold using an SVM trained on a training set (including a two-round SVM training for the pseudo-feedback feature).
Yes; they created their own dataset including 202 confirmed rumours obtained from Sina Weibo's official rumour debunking service (and 202 non-rumours plus 200 news articles from Xinhua).
Chinese
They define rumours as unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted news wire / official media.
They compared with LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
Generative document evaluation and document classification.
They evaluate on CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese), MSRA, Weibo, and Resume.

They introduce a relative positional encoding R_{t-j} = [ ... sin((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}) cos((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}) ... ]^T and compute attention scores as A^{rel}_{t,j} = Q_t K_j^T + Q_t R_{t-j}^T + u K_j^T + v R_{t-j}^T with Q,K,V = HW_q, H_{:d_k}, HW_v and learnable u,v. R_{t-j} encodes both distance and direction (sin terms change sign for forward vs backward offsets) so the Q_t R_{t-j}^T and v R_{t-j}^T terms introduce direction- and distance-awareness.

Yes — after adapting the Transformer, their model outperforms BiLSTM-based models and achieves state-of-the-art performance on six NER datasets among models without pre-trained language models or designed features.
Unanswerable
They evaluated on the MetaQA dataset.

The paper reports experiments with different word embeddings to initialize a state-of-the-art Bi-LSTM-CRF network for event detection and classification in Italian (following the EVENTI evaluation). It achieves a new state-of-the-art by improving F1 by 1.3 points for detection and 6.5 points for classification using a single-step approach, and shows that embeddings have a major impact on performance.
The paper uses the FBK-HLT system (Mirza and Minard, 2014), a cascade of two SVM classifiers based on rich linguistic features, as its baseline.
Unanswerable

They assign each cluster word to a WordNet synset by choosing S* that minimizes the total distance between synsets, generate candidate labels L as the union of all hypernyms of the synsets in S*, and rank those candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the synsets in S*, selecting the label with the least total distance.

They train domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings (w ∈ R100) for the gender-associated words and then apply k-means clustering to those embeddings, running k-means 50 times and keeping the model with the lowest sum of squared errors to avoid local optima.
The celebrity dataset has 15,917 texts (342,645 sentences) and the professor dataset has 283,973 texts (976,677 sentences).
They used the centroid of the cluster as a strong baseline label and a random baseline (20%) for the word-intrusion task.
They generally rank low and typically “play along” — producing flirtatious or retaliatory responses — while rarely giving polite refusals.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers.
Yes — the customised HeidelTime achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional.

Yes — the morphology-level model (morph/oracle) uses gold morphological tags including inflectional features (e.g., Case=Par;Number=Sing) and the paper explicitly analyzes derivational morphology by evaluating sentences containing derived words.
Gold morphological features consisting of the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags (with additional information such as parts-of-speech tags for Turkish).

They used the Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia, and the Flickr30K and COCO image-sentence matching benchmarks.
English
Unanswerable
They compared with five TQA baselines: random, BiDAF, text only (TQA1, based on MemoryNet), text+image (TQA2, VQA), and text+diagrams (TQA3, DSDP-NET).

Some of the data are domain-specific: Semantic Scholar and SciGraph are specific to the scientific domain, and the Textbook Question Answering corpus is from middle school science curricula. However, Wikipedia, Flickr30K and COCO provide general-purpose image-text data.
They were extracted from PDFs downloaded from the Semantic Scholar corpus using PDFFigures2.
Unanswerable
No, they did not leverage lexicons.
The Weka baseline (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a).
Their intra-sentence attention RNN outperformed the Weka baseline on the development dataset but performed worse than the baseline on the test dataset.

The training, validation and test datasets provided for the shared task (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a), consisting of tweets for four emotions (joy, sadness, fear, and anger) annotated using Best-Worst Scaling.
They state that hate speech "promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups" and "can incite hate crime."
English, French, and Arabic.

They indicate (a) whether the text is direct or indirect; (b) if it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; (c) the attribute based on which it discriminates against an individual or a group of people; (d) the name of this group; and (e) how the annotators feel about its content within a range of negative to neutral sentiments.
The final dataset is composed of a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language, and comparable corpora of 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets.

Semantic Web technologies can provide explicit semantic knowledge (Linked Data) to disambiguate words and real‑world entities and their relationships, improving lexical and syntactic disambiguation and named‑entity translation via techniques like WSD, NERD, SPARQL queries, reasoning and knowledge‑graph embeddings. They also enable using user/contextual data to handle non‑standard speech and translating/enriching knowledge bases to augment language models and improve MT quality and scalability.

Challenges include that SWT have mostly been applied only at the semantic analysis step and common application techniques (pre- and post-editing) are tedious to implement for common words and require repeated application; syntactic disambiguation lacks good solutions and reasoners suffer poor run-time performance. Additionally, named-entity recognition/disambiguation failures harm translation quality, non-standard speech and idioms remain open problems, KBs are often English-centric limiting cross-lingual benefit, and the use of multiple SW resources simultaneously has not yet been investigated.

Other obstacles include structural divergence (reordering errors), linguistic properties/features (tense, grammatical gender), and Lopez and Post’s five open problems: excessive focus on English/European languages (lack of low-resource research), SMT limitations across domains, translating non-standard social‑network speech, difficult translations among morphologically rich languages, and mismatched parallel data for speech translation.
Root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy.
Unanswerable
USC-TIMIT database

Yes — they compare weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning (Table 3) and Section 4.5 shows that a hybrid of matrix factorization and weight pruning is better than single solutions.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP and QNLI.
News articles and corresponding highlights from the DailyMail website.
DailyMail.
Yes — they include a neural abstractive baseline (NN-ABS) and report that their word-extraction model (NN-WE) consistently outperforms the purely abstractive model.

They are talking about ontologies of hierarchical data—transitive entailment relations such as IS-A (e.g., pit bull → dog → mammal) and background commonsense facts like a room may have a door and entering a room is generally through a door.

They mean using the geometric structure of embedding space—e.g., the coordinate-wise ordering enforced by order embeddings, where partial-order relations are encoded by requiring a general concept embedding to be smaller than a specific concept embedding in every coordinate—so that predictions automatically obey hierarchical/partial-order constraints.
Yes; they learned sentiment representations by training a bilateral LSTM on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.

Word unigrams and bigrams (TF–IDF), distributed semantics including a 300-dimensional averaged word2vec representation (also GloVe and Sent2Vec), and sentiment features from Empath’s 192 lexical categories plus a bilateral LSTM sentiment embedding trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.

They consist of diagrams which describe topics in elementary school natural sciences.
No — the AI2D annotations were created by crowd-sourced non-expert annotators, while the AI2D-RST annotations were produced by trained experts.

They are compared by two experiments that evaluate how effectively crowd-sourced and expert-annotated graphs can represent the multimodal structure of diagrams for representation learning using various graph neural networks; the results show that the identity of diagram elements can be learned from their layout features while the expert annotations provide better representations of diagram types.
Amazon Mechanical Turk

They opt for Recurrent Neural Networks (the Hierarchical Attention Network, HAN) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN).

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison and a new Wikipedia-derived dataset gathered from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” (2018 and 2009) by iteratively crawling “See also”, “References” and “External links” up to two hops from the seed list.
Unanswerable

They use Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” as seed pages and propagate labels by crawling “See also”, “References” and “External links” up to two hops (treating linked pages as weakly labeled controversial), with random Wikipedia articles used as negative seeds.

Yes — they compare their semantic neural approaches to lexical approaches, including language-model baselines (LM-DBPedia, LM-wiki), an SVM baseline, the TILE-Clique matching model, and a sentiment baseline.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos comprising a total of 1.2B tokens were used for training.
About 64 M segments comprising a total of 1.2 B tokens.
1500-dimensional frame-level visual features extracted from video frames at 1-second intervals.
Yes — the NMT LSTM models used the "scaled Luong" attention mechanism.
An LSTM-based neural machine translation model with (scaled) Luong attention, and the Transformer model.

Symbolic rewriting is transforming symbolic terms by applying rewrite rules (equations): given an equation s = t, in a context u and with a substitution θ a term u[θ(s)] is rewritten to u[θ(t)]. Examples in the paper include ground and nonground rewrites from Prover9 proofs and normalizing a polynomial term into a canonical form.

They let users specify anchor terms/variables and add a mutual-information bottleneck term β Σ_{(i,j)∈R} I(X_i;Y_j) to the CorEx objective to preserve information about these anchors, thereby seeding topics and imposing semantics on latent factors; anchors can be attached flexibly (multiple anchors to one factor or one anchor to multiple factors) and β=1 works well.
20 Newsgroups and i2b2 Obesity Challenge 2008.
No. They mention SeededLDA in Related Work, but their experiments compare Anchored CorEx to Unsupervised CorEx and a Naive Bayes baseline rather than to LDA.
Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese Social media.
Sina Weibo.
They used the Weibo NER corpus (a modified labelled corpus as in Peng and Dredze (2016)) and the same unlabeled Sina Weibo text (112,971,734 Weibo messages).
XNLI: Cross-lingual NLI; MLDoc: Document Classification; Cross-lingual Intent Classification; POS Tagging; Named Entity Recognition.
Yes: the mNMT model improves upon bilingual baselines for low-resource language pairs but performs worse than bilingual baselines on high-resource language pairs.
Yes — in the in-language experiments mBERT is fine-tuned as one model per language (they also report a joint one-model-for-all-languages setting).

They use a translation-based pipeline using the Google Translate API that translates the target-language query to English, passes it to the English Poly-encoder model, then translates the English response back to the target language (target query → English query → English response → target response).
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes — they automatically created a contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus.

They use as baselines a standard bidirectional RNN with attention (trained with Nematus; sentence-level, decoder input/output embeddings tied) and a standard context-agnostic Transformer (identical to a Transformer‑base in Vaswani et al. 2017).

Recurrent context-aware models: concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, s-hier-to-2 (with weight-tying variants); Transformer context-aware models: concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) context-aware Transformer (separate context encoder with shared layers).
English and German.

They use a simple EL algorithm that links each mention to the entity with the highest commonness score computed from Wikipedia anchor links (estimating the probability of an entity given only the mention string); this commonness score is also used as the confidence g in prediction, and they apply the Ganea and Hofmann (2017) heuristic to resolve coreferences of generic person mentions to more specific mentions within the same document.
They use two layers of BiLSTMs to encode context, concatenate context, mention string and KB type representations plus the EL score, and feed this into a three-layer MLP.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN.
One.
Precision, recall, F1-score, support, and accuracy (including accuracy without the ‘O’-tag).
The model gives an F1-score of 0.89 for the concept recognition task.
Yes. Implementation with demo and dataset is available at: https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

The BIO Labelling Scheme uses the following labels: abb — represents abbreviations such as TRL representing Technology Readiness Level; grp — represents a group of people or an individual such as Electrical Engineers, Systems Engineers or a Project Manager; syscon — represents any system concepts such as engineering unit, product, hardware, software (mostly physical concepts); opcon — represents operational concepts such as decision analysis process, technology maturity assessment, system requirements review; seterm — represents generic terms frequently used in SE text and those that do not fall under syscon or opcon such as project, mission, key performance parameter, audit; event — represents event-like information in SE text such as Pre-Phase A, Phase A, Phase B; org — represents an organization such as ‘NASA’, ‘aerospace industry’; art — represents names of artifacts or instruments such as ‘AS1300’; cardinal — represents numerical values such as ‘1’, ‘100’, ‘one’; loc — represents location-like entities such as component facilities or centralized facility; mea — represents measures, features, or behaviors such as cost, risk, or feasibility.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
Roughly 3700 sentences.
F-measure.
Position of sentence, sentence length, tense, content features, qualifying adjectives and meta-discourse features.
They used word2vec word embeddings (averaged for sentence vectors), paragraph vectors (ParaVec / PV-DM), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) inspired by sentiment-specific embeddings.
No; they used the existing Argumentative Zoning Corpus (80 AZ-annotated conference articles) as their test dataset.

Sentence embeddings were learned from a large text corpus using three models: (1) averaging the vectors of the words in a sentence where word vectors are learned by word2vec (AVGWvEC), (2) paragraph vectors (PV-DM / PARAVEC), and (3) averaging BAS-specific word embeddings trained in a sentiment-specific-word-embedding–inspired manner (BSWE).
Argumentative zoning (AZ) is rhetorical sentence classification: the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites (YouLikeHits and Like4Like) between February and April 2019, created dummy accounts to participate (with IRB approval) and recorded Tweet IDs of tweets posted for gaining retweets, then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects and the authors’ timelines.

They compare to state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods—Spam Detection 1 (Wu et al. [5]) and Spam Detection 2 (Rajdev et al. [12])—and feature-concatenation baselines using Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English
They focus on freemium blackmarket services, specifically social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.
They train a two-language (English/Chinese) and a three-language (English/French/Chinese) XNLG.
The decoder is a Transformer-based decoder as part of the sequence-to-sequence Transformer model (Vaswani et al. 2017).
The encoder is a bidirectional encoder based on the Transformer architecture.
XLM (Lample and Conneau 2019), a state-of-the-art cross-lingual pre-trained Transformer.

They use Amazon Mechanical Turk triple-pairing prompt/story matching (human judges select the correct pairing among three stories and prompts; 105 stories per model grouped into questions with 15 judges per question) and a separate blind pairwise human preference test comparing hierarchical versus non-hierarchical generation (400 pairs evaluated by 5 judges each).
Perplexity on the test set and prompt ranking accuracy.

They compare against: (1) Language Models — GCNN (gated convolutional language model) with and without added self-attention; (2) seq2seq — LSTMs and convolutional seq2seq architectures, including Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; (3) Ensemble — an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and (4) KNN — a TF-IDF/FASTTEXT + FAISS nearest-neighbor retrieval model (retrieving up to 150-word stories).

Fleiss’s Kappa

Yes — they computed Fleiss’s Kappa inter-annotator agreement and report values in Table 3 (aggression 0.23; repetition 0.18; harmful intent 0.42; visibility among peers 0.51; target power 0.37; author power 0.10; equal power 0.22; cyberbullying 0.18).
170 annotators.

They use low-complexity social-network measurements: five neighborhood-overlap features—down (downward overlap), up (upward overlap), in (inward overlap), out (outward overlap), and bi (bidirectional overlap)—computed as modified Jaccard similarities over followers/following sets; plus user-based metrics: friends and followers counts, verified status, and number of tweets in six-month timeline snapshots.
Aggressive, Repetitive, Intentional, Visible, and Imbalanced.
All available definitions contained a strict subset of the following criteria: aggression (AGGR), repetition (REP), harmful intent (HARM), visibility among peers (PEER), and power imbalance (POWER).

They evaluated similarity to the training data using Phrase Overlap and K-gram-Jaccard, checked syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser, and assessed humor content subjectively via human judgment.
The joke data came from CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.
They use filters of size 1 × 3.
They outperform by 0.105 in MRR (about 25.1% relative improvement) and by 6.1% absolute improvement in Hits@10 on FB15k-237.

Raw agreement (Phase 1: Scene .92, Function .95, Construal .90; Phase 2: Scene .93, Function .90, Construal .89) and Cohen’s kappa (Phase 1: Scene .90, Function .93, Construal .88; Phase 2: Scene .92, Function .88, Construal .88).

They manually tokenized (automatic tokenization with Jieba followed by manual corrections), manually identified adposition targets according to their criteria, and ran StanfordNLP to obtain POS tags and dependency trees which were stored alongside supersense annotations in CoNLL-U-Lex. The corpus was jointly annotated by three native Mandarin speakers: supersense labeling was performed cooperatively by the three for 25% of targets and independently for the remainder followed by cooperative adjudication, with inter-annotator agreement reported across two phases.
20,287 tokens.

They use the training dataset of about 20,800 samples (features: ID, title, author, text, label) and do not use the provided unlabeled test set; test data for evaluation is selected randomly from the training set.

The paper explores CountVectorizer, TF-IDFVectorizer, Word2Vec (as unsupervised pre-training/NLP models) and fine-tunes Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Long Short Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM).

Numerical features, language-model probabilities, word clusters (k-means on word embeddings), topic-model (LDA) document representations, part-of-speech tag n-gram features, and essay text bigram bag-of-words (idf-weighted).
The evaluation metrics were the challenge's custom error metric E and Accuracy.
Gradient boosted trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling as implemented in the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) toolkit.

Further analysis and validation to determine whether LDA's usefulness is due to its modeling power or an artifact of the dataset, specifically by releasing essay topics so that validation splits can be done on the basis of these topics.
A tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance.

By manual evaluation using the 5-point scale from Kocabiyikoglu et al. (2018) (1 = Wrong alignment; 2 = Partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence; 3 = Partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information; 4 = Correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information; 5 = Correct alignment and fully compositional translation) on samples split into three bins according to hunalign confidence score.

By manual evaluation: the audio-text alignment was rated on a 3-point scale (1 = wrong alignment; 2 = partial alignment, some words or sentences may be missing; 3 = correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at start or end) by two annotators who each rated 30 items from each confidence bin with 10 overlapping items to compute inter-annotator reliability.
Yes — it performs stably and promisingly even if only one sentence appears in a bag and thus keeps the consistency across all training examples.

We define hate speech as language that is used to express hatred towards a targeted group or is intended to be derogatory, to humiliate, or to insult the members of the group. In extreme cases this may also be language that threatens or incites violence, but limiting our definition only to such cases would exclude a large proportion of hate speech.
They train a logistic regression with L2 regularization (using a one-versus-rest framework).

By assigning each sample a weight inversely proportional to the estimated P(y|h) (estimated via K-fold cross-prediction) using w = 1/((1−ε)P(y|h)+ε) and normalizing weights across labels, the framework balances artifacts across classes (creating an artifact-balanced distribution) and thereby mitigates the bias pattern.
SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI and SICK.

The dataset was a sample of 10,417 users from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators manually labeled users as "bot" (users who do not appear to be posting personal information), "non-bot", or "unavailable" (sites not viewable due to privacy settings, removal, or suspension), with IAA κ = 0.93 on 1,000 overlapping annotations. After resolving disagreements, 413 (4%) were annotated as "bot", 7,849 (75.35%) as "non-bot", and the remainder (about 20%) as "unavailable".

By incorporating additional features and a statistical machine learning classifier into the existing system (originally designed for political bot detection) to improve performance on health-related Twitter users.
Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, and characterizing drug abuse.
Yes — they are from a customer support dataset sourced from Twitter.
The pre-trained version of BERT without special emoji tokens.
2000 tuples.
A customer support dataset of 2000 conversation tuples collected by Hori and Hori sourced from Twitter.
They used a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), an implementation of fast gradient boosting on decision trees.
Yes — they investigated state-of-the-art embedding methods, leveraging BERT and a domain-specific pre-trained embedding.

They created PICONET by collecting structured PubMed abstracts (search on April 11, 2019 yielding 363,078 structured abstracts) filtered to Article Types=Clinical Trial, Species=Humans, Language=English, then lemmatizing and clustering abstract section labels to choose headings representative of P/I/O, manually inspecting samples to select candidate labels, extracting full abstract sections (not splitting sentences), adding negative examples from headings like "aim", and cleaning/filtering (language identification, removing missing values, cleaning unicode, and keeping sequences of 5–200 words).
It was automatically labeled.

They suffered from redundancy and ambiguity, relied on keyword-based labeling that produced sections mixing P/I/O (e.g., population sections containing methods or interventions) and, in Jin and Szolovits (2018), split multi-sentence sections into multiple sentences which lost context and produced poor candidates.
The document reports that both single-language and multilingual BERT capture syntax-sensitive agreement patterns well, and does not state that single-language BERT outperforms multilingual BERT.
They explore subject–verb agreement, noun–determiner agreement, noun–attributive-adjective agreement, and subject–predicate-adjective agreement.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
word-LSTM, word-CNN and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.

The baselines are All Sarcasm (assumes every instance is sarcastic) and Random (randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic).
Twitter and Amazon reviews.

Training data came from two domains: Twitter (tweets collected Feb–Mar 2016 labeled via trailing #sarcasm and six emotion hashtags as non-sarcastic) and Amazon product reviews from the publicly available sarcasm corpus by Filatova (2012); both datasets were randomly divided into training (80%) and testing (20%).
The best model, LSTM and GRU with Attention, achieved Not Offensive F1 0.96, Offensive F1 0.77, weighted average F1 0.88 and macro-F1 0.89.

They tested five classical models—SVM (linear), SVM (RBF), Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier (SGDC), Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes—and deep models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.

Yes — they hypothesize that because MagiCoder performs pure syntactical (word-by-word, up to stemming) recognition and does not use sentence structure or semantics, it works well when Italian ADR descriptions are short and typically follow a simple "name of the pathology + location/adjective" form, while longer descriptions introduce synonyms, extra words/connectives and noise that hinder matching.

1. Definition of ad hoc data structures; 2. Preprocessing of the original text; 3. Word-by-word linear scan of the description and "voting task"; 4. Weights calculation; 5. Sorting of voted terms and winning terms release.

By building hash-based meta-dictionaries (DictByWord and DictByWordStem) that map words/stems to LLTs in O(km) and then performing a single word-by-word linear scan of the narrative that "votes" terms using constant-time hash lookups, so the main processing is linear in the narrative length and proportional to the terminology size.

They evaluate script event prediction on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset following Li et al. (2018a), using the SGNN framework and replacing SGNN's input event embeddings with their intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset (small: 230 event pairs from Weber et al., 2018; a big dataset annotated by the authors with 2,000 event pairs), the transitive sentence similarity dataset (Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh, 2014), the multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset (Granroth-Wilding and Clark, 2016) for script event prediction, and a stock market prediction dataset based on the S&P 500 index (news events). It also leverages the Event2Mind (Rashkin et al., 2018) and ATOMIC (Sap et al., 2019) commonsense knowledge bases for intent and sentiment information.

They achieve a 10% relative accuracy increase over the baseline, with the two-stage system giving 88.88% overall (87.72% ≤3s, 90.04% >3s) versus the baseline 78.85% (77.60% ≤3s, 80.10% >3s), and very high per-dialect accuracies for some dialects (e.g., Shanghai 98.8%, Hefei 99.8%).

There is no clear winner: MODULAR is better at reducing bias and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT produces more fluent responses, preserves meaning better, and has higher BLEU.
They were edits made by English Wikipedia editors (the Wiki community), not by trained annotators.

They define neutralizing as giving an input sentence and producing an output whose meaning is as similar as possible but with subjective bias removed. They propose two algorithms: MODULAR, which uses a BERT-based detection module to tag biased words and an LSTM-based editing module joined via a gated join embedding to edit the sentence; and CONCURRENT, which directly encodes the source with BERT and decodes a neutralized sentence with an attentional LSTM (with copy and coverage) trained as a subjectivity-to-neutral translation.
Recognizing American Sign Language (ASL) word-level signs (51 classes) using multimodal data: RGB video and Kinect skeletal (3D joint) data.
